preamble: The palatalized voiced alveolar fricative ([zʲ]) often undergoes a shift to the voiced postalveolar fricative ([ʒ]). This transformation is a natural progression where the tongue, already raised towards the hard palate for the palatalized alveolar articulation, retracts slightly and broadens its constriction, moving the point of articulation from the alveolar ridge to the postalveolar region.
phoneticEffects: Palatalization, postalveolarization, assimilation of place of articulation

examples.note: The intervocalic /tʲ/ in Latin ratiōne(m) underwent a series of changes in the proto-Romance period, including weakening to /ʣʲ/ then /zʲ/, which subsequently developed into /ʒ/ in Old French.

This shift is an example of yod-coalescence, where an alveolar /z/ followed by a palatal approximant /j/ (phonetically similar to a palatalized /zʲ/) coalesces into a voiced postalveolar fricative /ʒ/.

sources: Does [z] + [j] equal [ ʒ ]? - English Stack Exchange. (April 13 2021).
